reasoning: The injustice is obvious because the rule is being enforced against the user while identical, visible violations by neighbors are explicitly ignored by the same authority figure. A reasonable person would immediately agree that selective enforcement based on personal animus or arbitrary targeting is unfair.

Applying a grading penalty retroactively to absences that occurred when the syllabus explicitly stated attendance was optional is a clear violation of procedural fairness. No reasonable person would defend failing a student for relying on the stated rules of the course.

A policy that allows an automated system to issue a binding failure for environmental noise without any possibility of human review is procedurally unfair on its face. No reasonable person would defend a system where a professor refuses to verify whether a 'suspicious audio' flag was actually cheating or just a lawnmower.

The enforcement disparity is stark and irrational: the authority punishes minor cosmetic issues (streaks) while ignoring major safety hazards (chemical spills) committed by others. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that penalizing the safer student while letting dangerous behavior slide, coupled with refusing to provide grading criteria, constitutes unfair targeting.

The application of the rule is obviously unjust because compliance is physically impossible due to the parent's verified military deployment on a submarine. A reasonable person would agree that refusing to accept deployment papers as a temporary exception, while threatening to drop the student's classes, constitutes an absurd and indefensible bureaucratic failure.

The injustice is obvious because the enforcers (RAs) are actively participating in flagrant violations with a privileged group while strictly punishing the user for a minor infraction. A reasonable person would immediately recognize this double standard and corruption of authority as unfair application.

The application of the rule creates a genuine impossibility: the student cannot obtain a signature from abusive parents they are estranged from. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that enforcing a parental signature requirement—while rejecting the standard mechanism (dependency override) designed for exactly this situation—is bureaucratically absurd and unjust.

The case presents a clear instance of selective enforcement where the user is targeted daily while identical violations by peers in the immediate vicinity are ignored. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that enforcing a rule against only one specific individual, while knowingly permitting others to break it, constitutes unfair targeting and harassment.

Applying a new disciplinary rule retroactively to conduct that was compliant at the time is a textbook example of procedural injustice. A reasonable person would immediately agree that expelling a student for photos that were permitted when posted violates basic principles of fairness and due process.

The injustice is obvious because the school imposes the maximum penalty (expulsion) for a technical violation (container type) while explicitly refusing to verify the student's legal medical claim. A reasonable person would agree that refusing to look at exculpatory evidence (a doctor's verification) before destroying a student's academic career is procedurally unfair.
